mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 760i / 760Li engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 760i/760Li engine typically costs £5,500–£15,000 supply only depending on the generation. The N74B60A V12 costs on average £12,499, with new or reconditioned engines up to £14,694. Fitting adds £1,200–£2,500 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

It depends on which generation you're asking about. The N73 (E65/E66) is complex and expensive — HPFP failure, coolant pipe leaks, oil leaks, Valvetronic/VANOS issues, and timing chain stretch are well-documented. The N74 (F01/F02) is more reliable but still has HPFP, water pump, turbo, and oil leak issues. The G70's S68 V8 is too new for long-term data but shares the 'hot V' complexity of the N63 family. Across all, meticulous maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 760i / 760Li?

acceptedAnswer.text: N73 HPFP failure (E65/E66), N73/N74 coolant transfer pipe and cooling system leaks, oil leaks from valve covers and oil filter housing (all generations), N73 Valvetronic/VANOS issues, N74 turbocharger wear (F01/F02), and N73 timing chain stretch. See the Era Map above for generation-specific advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a 760i / 760Li engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-maintained F01 760i (values £8,000–£20,000+ depending on condition and mileage), an £8,000–£12,000 reconditioned engine is 40–100% of the vehicle's value — often only worth it for exceptional examples. Against a G70 (values £80,000+), a £10,000–£15,000 engine replacement is 10–20% — worth it. The BMW scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 760i / 760Li engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block. As a rule of thumb: E65/E66 (2003–2008) = N73B60; F01/F02 (2009–2015) = N74B60A; G70 (2023–present) = S68. Check your VIN plate and vehicle documentation for confirmation.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N73 HPFP, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: N73 HPFP failure is repairable without replacing the engine. HPFP replacement costs £800–£2,000. However, if HPFP failure has led to fuel contamination or other issues, more extensive work may be required. At the first sign of long cranking or misfires, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the 760i/760Li's engine eras. N74 V12 engines command the highest prices due to their complexity and rarity. A replacement N74B60A motor costs on average £12,499, with new or reconditioned engines up to £14,694. Used N74 complete engines with ~95k miles are listed at £13,299.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 760i/760Li engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £85–£130/hr (independent specialist) or £140–£200/hr (London/SE dealer). The V12's complexity and tight engine bay add significant labour. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

problems.title: N73 High-Pressure Fuel Pump (HPFP) Failure (E65/E66)
problems.symptoms: Long cranking before starting, misfires, power loss, stalling, engine warning light, rough running.
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£10,000 (if fuel system failure leads to engine damage)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you experience stalling or extended cranking, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The N73's high-pressure fuel system is its most notorious weak point. DI brings benefits, but it also brings high-pressure components that can be failure-prone with age. Symptoms include long cranking, misfires, power loss, or stalling. If you experience any of these, have the HPFP inspected immediately. Replacing the pump before it fails completely can prevent damage to other fuel system components. Given the complexity and cost of the N73, preventative maintenance is essential.

problems.title: N73 / N74 Coolant Transfer Pipe & Cooling System Leaks (E65/E66, F01/F02)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, sweet smell from engine bay, low coolant warning, overheating, coolant leaking from the weep hole in the front timing cover.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice coolant loss or overheating, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: BMW V12 models fitted with the N73 engine often suffer from a failure of the front seal of the water transfer feed pipe, which is located under the intake valley pan. Symptoms will initially be a coolant leak from the weep hole in the front timing cover, followed by loss of coolant, overheating, and possible engine failure. There's enough demand for dedicated repair solutions that aftermarket kits are available. The N74 also suffers from water pump leaks. If you notice coolant loss, have the system pressure-tested immediately.

problems.title: Oil Leaks — Valve Cover Gaskets & Oil Filter Housing (All Generations)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, visible oil seepage around the valve covers or oil filter housing, low oil level warning.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless oil starvation leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; oil leaks can damage other components

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are one of the most common problems across all 760i/760Li generations. On the N73 and N74, leaks from the valve cover gaskets and oil filter housing are particularly common. Over time, these gaskets can wear out, leading to leaks that can affect engine performance. The N73 also has a tendency to leaks and oil consumption. If you notice oil spots under the car or a burning smell, have it inspected promptly.

problems.title: N73 Valvetronic & VANOS System Issues (E65/E66)
problems.symptoms: Vibrations at low speeds, rough idle, hesitation on acceleration, reduced power, engine warning light.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can affect variable valve timing and lift

problems.recommendation: The N73 features Valvetronic variable valve lift paired with Double VANOS — a rare combination on a V12. However, the Double VANOS system has a modest resource here, sometimes less than 150,000 km. Vibrations at low speeds due to a malfunction of Valvetronic are common. If you experience rough idling or low-speed vibrations, have the Valvetronic and VANOS systems inspected.

problems.title: N74 Turbocharger Wear (F01/F02)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, whistling noise from the engine, excessive exhaust smoke, reduced fuel economy, engine warning light.
problems.replacementCost: £8,000–£14,000 (if turbo failure sends debris into the engine)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear whistling or experience power loss, book an inspection immediately

problems.recommendation: The N74's twin-turbo setup is a highlight, but it can also be a weak point. Turbines can fail up to 100,000 km due to coking in oil lines. Turbocharger failures can be expensive to repair and, if debris enters the engine, can lead to catastrophic failure. Regular oil changes with the correct BMW-approved oil and allowing the engine to cool after hard driving are the best prevention. If you notice whistling or power loss, have the turbos inspected immediately.

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch (N73)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the engine, engine warning light, loss of power, potential engine failure if chain skips or snaps.
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£10,000 (if chain snaps and valves meet pistons)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: After 150,000 km of run, timing chains on the N73 often stretch and require replacement. A cold-start rattle is the early warning sign — ignore it and you risk piston-valve contact and a full engine replacement. Regular oil changes with the correct BMW-approved oil are the best prevention.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 760i / 760Li? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using BMW-approved oil
* N73: HPFP replaced or functioning correctly (no long cranking, no misfires)
* N73: Coolant transfer pipe replaced with updated part (critical)
* N74: No turbo noise or power loss
* All generations: No oil leaks from valve covers or oil filter housing
* All generations: No coolant loss or overheating history
avoidIf: * No proof of regular maintenance — these flagship engines are extraordinarily expensive to repair
* N73 with HPFP issues — expensive repair ahead
* Any coolant loss with no diagnosis — coolant transfer pipe work is labour-intensive
* N73 with Valvetronic/VANOS issues — complex and costly
* N74 with turbo noise or power loss — turbo replacement is very expensive
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — these engines can hide extremely expensive faults
* "Cheap" examples — the purchase price is the least of your worries

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The BMW 760i/760Li represents the absolute pinnacle of BMW engineering — a V12-powered flagship limousine that defines luxury motoring. The N73 is a naturally aspirated masterpiece but one that demands deep pockets and careful maintenance. The N74 is more reliable but still complex and expensive. The G70 S68 V8 marks the end of an era, replacing the V12 with a hybridised V8. Across all generations, one rule applies: buy the best example you can afford, maintain it meticulously, and budget for significant maintenance costs. These are not cars for the faint of heart or light of wallet. As one owner's experience with a $4,500 760i shows — "some leaks, some misfires and generally easy to remedy problems" can quickly escalate.

cta.label: → Compare 760i/760Li engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 760i or 760Li (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Any rattles? (N73 timing chain) Any extended cranking? (N73 HPFP)
* Service History — Regular oil changes with BMW-approved oil are absolutely essential
* HPFP Health (N73) — Any history of HPFP replacement? Any long cranking or misfires?
* Coolant Level — Check cold and hot; any loss indicates potential coolant transfer pipe or water pump issues
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil around the valve covers and oil filter housing
* Valvetronic/VANOS (N73) — Any vibrations at low speeds? Rough idle?
* Turbo Health (N74) — Any whistling, power loss, or smoke? (turbo wear warning)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? HPFP, VANOS, or cooling system codes are most common

items.label: SECTION 8 — FAQ
items.value: 1. How much does a BMW 760i / 760Li engine replacement cost in the UK?

items.label: A reconditioned 760i/760Li engine typically costs £5,500–£15,000 supply only depending on the generation. The N74B60A V12 costs on average £12,499, with new or reconditioned engines up to £14,694. Fitting adds £1,200–£2,500 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

It depends on which generation you're asking about. The N73 (E65/E66) is complex and expensive — HPFP failure, coolant pipe leaks, oil leaks, Valvetronic/VANOS issues, and timing chain stretch are well-documented. The N74 (F01/F02) is more reliable but still has HPFP, water pump, turbo, and oil leak issues. The G70's S68 V8 is too new for long-term data but shares the 'hot V' complexity of the N63 family. Across all, meticulous maintenance is essential.
items.value: 3. What are the common problems with the BMW 760i / 760Li?

items.label: N73 HPFP failure (E65/E66), N73/N74 coolant transfer pipe and cooling system leaks, oil leaks from valve covers and oil filter housing (all generations), N73 Valvetronic/VANOS issues, N74 turbocharger wear (F01/F02), and N73 timing chain stretch. See the Era Map above for generation-specific advice.
items.value: 4. Is it worth replacing a 760i / 760Li engine, or should I scrap the car?

items.label: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-maintained F01 760i (values £8,000–£20,000+ depending on condition and mileage), an £8,000–£12,000 reconditioned engine is 40–100% of the vehicle's value — often only worth it for exceptional examples. Against a G70 (values £80,000+), a £10,000–£15,000 engine replacement is 10–20% — worth it. The BMW scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing. For E65/E66 cars, values are often low enough that engine replacement is rarely financially justifiable unless the car is immaculate or has sentimental value.
items.value: 5. How do I find my BMW 760i / 760Li engine code?

items.label: The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block. As a rule of thumb: E65/E66 (2003–2008) = N73B60; F01/F02 (2009–2015) = N74B60A; G70 (2023–present) = S68. Check your VIN plate and vehicle documentation for confirmation.
items.value: 6. Can I repair the N73 HPFP, or does it require engine replacement?

items.label: N73 HPFP failure is repairable without replacing the engine. HPFP replacement costs £800–£2,000. However, if HPFP failure has led to fuel contamination or other issues, more extensive work may be required. At the first sign of long cranking or misfires, book an inspection immediately.
items.value: Disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data from our network of engine specialists. Actual costs vary by variant, engine code, damage severity and workshop location. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes. Every engine supplied through EnginesMarket carries a minimum 12-month unlimited-mileage warranty — always confirm terms with your chosen supplier.
